coredata.dc:title: Precise optical engineering of carbon-based photoluminescence arrays based on e-beam irradiation process
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Kaixi
Mei, Linyu
Bi, Xiaoxue
Chen, Yiqin
Niu, Yaokai
Ji, Zhiqiang
Geng, Wenping
Han, Shuqi
Niu, Liya
Chou, Xiujian
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbonnanomaterials have been widely studied for their low biological toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility, unique physical–chemical properties and obvious PL properties. The reliable fabrication of high-resolution fluorescence arrays has important significance in further studies and applications. The organic resist can be in-situ transferred into specific carbon-based micro-nano structure with fluorescence properties under e-beam irradiation. In this work, polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) thin film was adopted for preparing solid-carbon nanostructures with 23.89 nm feature size. In a dark field environment, the structure has presented broad luminescent spectrum with maximum at 600 nm. Considering the significant influence of the surface molecular state on solid-carbon arrays, ammonia solution was used to enhance the PL intensity of the sample. The results verified that PL intensity was enhanced by a factor of 17 before and after ammonia solution treatment. Then carbon-based nanostructures were also fabricated on the surface of MoS2 film for studying PL properties between 2D materials and carbon-based dot arrays. The results show that superposition state was produced based on PL intensity and peak location of the solid-carbon dot array and MoS2 film. The optical behavior study of high-resolution solid-carbon arrays will provide a powerful supplement for carbon-based photophysical study and high-performance sensing devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based photoluminescence
E-beam irradiation
High-resolution
NH3·H2O
MoS2 film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fabrication process of PMMA fluorescence nanometer arrays based on e-beam lithography process. (b) The molecular formula of PMMA. (c) Contrast curve of PMMA negative resist for fluorescence arrays.

(a-d) SEM images of irradiated negative PMMA resist nanostructure. All scale bar in the images is 360 nm. (e-h) AFM images of corresponding nanostructures in (a-d). The height variation is exhibited by the mapping image.

(a) Optical image of irradiated PMMA rectangle structures. (b) Raman spectrum was tested for the cross-linked PMMA area. (c, d) The discrete peaks are fitted by Origin software. (e) C1s HR-XPS spectra of the irradiated PMMA film. (f) The Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy spectra of the PMMA.

(a) PL spectra test of PMMA irradiated by different exposure doses varying from 100 μC/cm2 to 6000 μC/cm2. (b) PL spectra test of irradiated PMMA circles with different sizes. (c) Optical images of fluorescence irradiated-PMMA structure at different times.

(a) The absorption, PL excitation and PL spectra of graphitized carbon-based nanostructure. (b) PL test spectra of irradiated PMMA structure with or without NH3·H2O modification after 1 day, 3 days and 5 days. The insert image in test spectra is an optical image of irradiated PMMA in dark filed model. (c) PL mapping image of irradiated PMMA decorated with NH3·H2O solution.

(a) Optical image of irradiated PMMA dot arrays prepared on the surface of MoS2 film. (b) PL mapping image of sample mentioned in (a). (c) The related PL test spectra of pure MoS2, irradiated PMMA, and irradiated PMMA/MoS2 composite structure.

head.ce:title: Precise optical engineering of carbon-based photoluminescence arrays based on e-beam irradiation process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Semiconductors and Physics, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Semiconductors and Physics, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of National Defense Science and Technology on Electronic Measurement, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of National Defense Science and Technology on Electronic Measurement

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of National Defense Science and Technology on Electronic Measurement, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent carbonnanomaterials have been widely studied for their low biological toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility, unique physical–chemical properties and obvious PL properties. The reliable fabrication of high-resolution fluorescence arrays has important significance in further studies and applications. The organic resist can be in-situ transferred into specific carbon-based micro-nano structure with fluorescence properties under e-beam irradiation. In this work, polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) thin film was adopted for preparing solid-carbon nanostructures with 23.89 nm feature size. In a dark field environment, the structure has presented broad luminescent spectrum with maximum at 600 nm. Considering the significant influence of the surface molecular state on solid-carbon arrays, ammonia solution was used to enhance the PL intensity of the sample. The results verified that PL intensity was enhanced by a factor of 17 before and after ammonia solution treatment. Then carbon-based nanostructures were also fabricated on the surface of MoS2 film for studying PL properties between 2D materials and carbon-based dot arrays. The results show that superposition state was produced based on PL intensity and peak location of the solid-carbon dot array and MoS2 film. The optical behavior study of high-resolution solid-carbon arrays will provide a powerful supplement for carbon-based photophysical study and high-performance sensing devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based fluorescent materials (CFMs) exhibit a huge potential in various applications including organic light-emitting diodes, cell selection, data recording and information printing due to their excellent biocompatibility and high photostability [1–5]. The luminescence properties are closely related to structure size, spatial distribution, and elementary composition of CFMs. How to precisely control spatial resolution and elementary composition has become a research hotspot for carbon-based fluorescence study [6–8]. Extensive works were explored to promote the reliable preparation of carbon-based photoluminescence materials in micro-nano scale structure preparation.

Generally, the fabrication process can be divided into bottom-up and top-down methods. The bottom-up methods usually consisted of microwave synthesis, combustion reactions, hydrothermal synthesis, and so on [9–15]. The methods were widely used for high-quality and mass production due to their low-cost precursors and simple operation steps. Zhang et al. exhibited carbon dots with strong blue photoluminescence one-step microwave-assisted route using citric acid (CA) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as precursors. Benefiting from its high biocompatibility, carbon dots were also used for the detection of H2O2. Peng. et al. synthesized graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots through a green and facile one-step hydrothermal approach. The average sizes of carbon dots were 4.1, 4.6 and 6.3 nm, and the corresponding luminescent quantum yield was 40 %. The top-down methods usually consisted of laser ablation, 3D printing, ion etching, and so on. The methods have obvious advantages such as better manufacturing accuracy with micro/nano dimension, more precise spatial control, and selective ability, which is useful for constructing high-performance micro/nano optical devices [16–20]. Zarzar et al. have reported a direct laser writing method to synthesize and pattern graphitic carbon from liquid organic precursors, which exhibited a paracrystalline-to-polycrystalline structure with microscale resolution. Peng et al. studied an ink-based 3D printing method to fabricate 3D biomimetric hierarchical graphene materials. The reductive graphene oxide sheets were first mixed in printable ink. By combining the printing process with freeze casting treatment, pr-GO was able to form microscopic cellular structures. Microscale carbon-based structures were much larger than photoluminescence wavelength, which limited the development of optical integration and application. Among top-down fabrication methods, e-beam lithography can draw custom patterns with sub-10 nm resolution because of breaking the optical diffraction limit. Combined with functional film deposition and lift-off process, the fluorescence structure could be decreased to a nanometer dimension. The nanofabrication ability of e-beam lithography will open the way to finish nano-optical integration and further application [21–24]. MoS2 (molybdenum disulfide) film is a typical two-dimensional nanomaterial, and its optical properties are related to the thickness. The fluorescence intensity is inversely proportional to the number of layers of MoS2 film. Two fluorescence characteristic peaks will be generated at ∼ 620 nm and ∼ 670 nm when excited by external laser source. Benefiting from its excellent photoelectric performance and fluorescence characteristics, MoS2 film is commonly combined with graphitized carbon materials to construct heterojunction. Combing MoS2 film with carbon-based fluorescent nanostructure has significance in various fields including fluorescent display, sensing and so on [25–28].

In this work, we have directly written carbon-based fluorescent nanostructure arrays by e-beam irradiation technology on PMMA organic thin film spin-coated on the surface of SiO2/Si substrate. Because the PMMA could be transferred into a graphitized carbon structure by the e-beam irradiation process, irradiated PMMA can be considered as a graphitized carbon-based nanostructure. The characterized size of irradiated PMMA nanostructure is as low as 23.98 nm, and the corresponding longitudinal roughness is less than 5 nm. The Raman characterization shows that the e-beam irradiation will promote the formation of graphitized carbon with oxygen and hydrogen functional groups. An obvious fluorescent phenomenon and luminescent spectrum with maximum at 600 nm were tested in the dark field model with excitation at 532 nm and 457 nm laser. We also carried out the related study of ammonia doping, which verifying the photoluminescence enhancement mechanism of N-doping graphitic carbon nanostructure. By combing MoS2 film with irradiated-PMMA nanostructure to construct fluorescent heterostructure, the enhancement of fluorescent peak was also clearly shown in photoluminescence mapping image. The enhancement of fluorescent peak was produced in heterostructure. Considering the detailed and systematic study process, this work will offer a great promise to fabricate light-emitting CFMs and photonic crystals in a space-resolved manner.

PMMA resist was purchased from Suzhou Research Material Company. It has a molecular weight of 950 K and is used for high-resolution e-beam lithography. The concentration of PMMA solution is 6 %, which makes it easy to acquire PMMA film with 50 ∼ 200 nm thickness by tuning the speed of the homogenizer. MoS2 film was prepared by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method. The length of the MoS2 sample side is about 10 μm. Ammonia solution with 30 % concentration was purchased from Chengdu Chron Chemical Co., Ltd. Then we submerged the graphitized carbon sample in a beaker containing the solution. Gently stirring method was adopted to accelerate the reaction between sample and ammonia solution. Following these steps, the sample was taken out and blown dry with a nitrogen gun for subsequent fluorescence spectra detection in 10 min. It is convenient to draw and drip on the surface of the functional structure.

The required exposure structure can be directly drawn by the layout software. Required exposure pattern can be directly drawn according to the layout software, and E-beam lithography equipment readout the layout software information to selectively expose the PMMA film in serial mode. In e-beam lithography process, electron beam was firstly extracted to the surface of the sample for irradiation at 30 kV traction voltage. Then internal oxygen/hydrogen atoms of PMMA are bombarded by the electron beam and escape from the surface of the sample, and the residual carbon atoms will further aggregate and recombine into graphitized carbon structure. The 30 μm aperture was selected for high precision fabrication, and the measured beam current is ∼ 316pA. Due to the high requirement of structural machining accuracy, all manufacturing processes are completed in a single field with 200 × 200 μm2 area. After exposure, the sample was soaked in acetone solution for 2 mins to obtain the micro-nano structure, and then the surface of the sample was cleaned with deionized water, and the water stain on the sample surface was dried with a nitrogen gun.

The morphology was characterized by SEM equipment (Zeiss Gemini S360) with the ability to focus the electron beam. A high-resolution image around the nanometer scale can be delivered for further analysis and observation. SEM is mostly used for the observation of planar construction. AFM is another useful tool for measuring the roughness of structure in longitudinal dimensions. The AFM equipment in this work also exhibits resolution around the nanometer scale. Its scanning area can reach 100 μm × 100 μm size, which can meet the requirements of e-beam irradiation of PMMA nanostructure.

Raman spectra test was done with the help of a confocal Raman imaging system (WITec alpha 300R). The spectra can be collected in high speed (≈1 s) and small test steps, and it can avoid the additional damage from laser irradiation in the characterization process. Photoluminescence spectrum and image is also taken by the Raman light path system. A small gating and dark field model was set for the collection of optical signals.

PMMA is an industry-standard positive resist and negative resist under different exposure doses. It exhibits high-resolution features and is used to study the lift applications of various nanodevices. In our patterning concept (Fig. 1 a), the PMMA was directly written by focused e-beam to fabricate various nanometer arrays. Its molecular formula is also provided in Fig. 1b. As the solid organic film, a cross-linking reaction was produced in the original PMMA resist under high e-beam irradiation. The irradiated PMMA nanostructure will produce a light-emitting phenomenon, i.e., a new wide PL peak can be seen under another incident laser with a different wavelength. By collecting of the height of PMMA film under different exposure doses, we have first investigated the contrast curve of as-exposed PMMA resist for fluorescence application. In this test curve, D0 means the minimal dose to obtain the visible structure, and D100 is the saturated dose to acquire a stable carbon-based structure. The contrast value is 1.25 based on γ = [log(D100/D0)] computational formula. The high statistics of the test data reflect the high precision manufacturing capability for subsequent fabrication and applications.

Considering the huge potential of PMMA resist in nanofabrication for the nano fluorescence array applications. Diverse and highly accurate solid carbon-based nanostructure should be constructed to satisfy the requirements of sensing applications. As shown in Fig. 2 (a-d), a series of nanoscale structures were exhibited. The minimum exposure PMMA structure size is 23.89 nm in a honeycomb hexagonal grid array. The lines in the whole array are smooth and uniform, which demonstrates a high precision control capability. Some other patterns including triangle, concentric annulus, and vertical grid structures were also fabricated to verify the potential for high spatial resolution optical applications. Besides x-y plane observation with the help of SEM equipment. The corresponding longitudinal roughness is also investigated with the help of the AFM characterization instrument. The test height mapping and curves were provided in Fig. 2 (e-h), in which ∼ 60 nm was reached. An average fluctuation of longitudinal dimension is about 5 nm. Compared with a lateral size of hundreds of nanometers in length, it is excellent and smooth for space position control [29–31].

Besides precise control of structure size, another important factor for fluorescence application is the chemical component and shape of the molecular chain. Raman characterization is a convenient tool for the analysis of carbon-based materials [32,33]. Then we collect Raman signals from irradiated PMMA film with different exposure doses. It is found that three characteristic peaks were located at 1380 cm−1, 1629 cm−1, and 2927 cm−1 positions, which is different from Raman peaks of graphene (D peak: 1350 cm−1, G peak: 1580 cm−1, 2D peak: 2690 cm−1). The results acquired by the Raman system were calibrated with a standard silicon sample before the test, so the dates are accurate. After carefully checking references, we find that the Raman peaks of irradiated PMMA are similar to Raman peaks of oxidized graphite. Generally, the Raman peak of perfect graphene crystal film is Gaussian distribution with a narrow FWHM (full width at half maxima) value. The peaks at 1629 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 positions were non-Gaussian distributions with ∼ 260 cm−1 FWHM value. In order to study the Raman test results, the 1629 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 peaks were divided into two Gaussian peaks, i.e. G + D model and the D + G model, respectively. The corresponding vibration is shown in Fig. 3 e, which means the formation of a honeycomb hexagonal graphene-like structure. The ID/IG ratio is about 2 in Fig. 3b, which means some defects exist in the sample. The obvious blue shift of characteristic peaks indicates that the irradiated PMMA is more similar to graphite than monolayer or few-layer graphene [34–36].

The chemical structure changes can be investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) transmission spectroscopy [37,38]. An obvious C1s peak was presented in Fig. 3e. The atomic percentage of carbon elements was changed from 33.3 % to 82.61 % according to the XPS peak table, which implied that more carbon atoms were retained in the e-beam irradiation process. By further peak-differentiating and imitating, the C1 peak was divided into two peaks located at 284.6 eV and 285.0 eV respectively. The results verified C–C and C = C chemical bonds of the graphitic structure were produced in irradiated PMMA samples. In the FTIR characterization process, four obvious characterize peaks were presented at 1408.1 cm−1, 1620.5 cm−1, 2900 cm−1, and 3363 cm−1, which correspond to the formation of covalent bonds including O–H, C = C, C–H, and O–H. The peaks that existed in the FTIR spectrum are similar to the results of the oxidized graphene sample. The C = C peak located at 1620.5 cm−1 may resulted from residual carbon atom honeycomb hexagon structure. Oxygen and hydrogen elements still existed because of substrate limitation and initial chemical bond energy.

Surface chemical bonds and molecular states are common ways to control the energy band of graphene for photoluminescence and electrical applications. Then the existence of an oxygen-containing functional group is useful to open up the band gap of a graphene-like structure, which leads to an obvious fluorescence phenomenon from photon absorption and radiation. The conjugated π system and the surface chemical group have been regarded as the main reasons for the fluorescence of carbon-based materials. The carbonization and surface defect state can be controlled by adopting different e-beam exposure doses. A higher degree of carbonization makes it easy to acquire a larger conjugated π system and low defect group, which has an important influence on the efficiency of electron-hole recombination [39–42]. Then will further change the fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 4 a, higher light intensity was presented because of a higher carbonization degree and larger π system area for recombination of electron-hole. The concentration-dependent fluorescence behavior is a common phenomenon in carbon dots solution. It deserves to explore the relationship between fluorescence intensity and material quantity in solid-carbon-based materials. Then we designed and fabricated different sizes of irradiated PMMA structures for the study of fluorescence properties. The PL spectra were provided in Fig. 4b, in which the light intensity is positively associated with structure size. The results reflect an obvious quantity dependence relation.

Besides the spectra, a series of optical images have also been taken. We found that the PL intensity of irradiated PMMA structure presented an obvious change from higher light intensity to lower light intensity. The reason for the phenomenon may be the heating effect from the radiant laser. In the initial state, the electron in the valence band of solid-carbon-based materials was excited to a conductive band receiving energy from the radiant laser. Then electrons in the valence band backed down to the ground band. And the sample can produce PL phenomenon. However, the radiant laser will produce external heat on carbon-based materials, which form oxygen-containing functional groups including C-O, C = O, and so on. The functional group will promote nonradiative jumping for electrons. As a result of that, the PL quenching was yielded, which led to the gradually darkening light intensity.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of the graphitized carbon-based nanostructure was displayed in Fig. 5 a. It is can be seen from that there is an obvious absorption peak at ∼ 382 nm, which is consistent with the excitation wavelength. The wide absorption spectrum means abundant energy band levels and electronic transitions for carbon-based nanostructure. In UV–vis test process, the PL emission wavelength with central peak at ∼ 584 nm. The blue-shift phenomenon of fluorescence peak may be resulted from excitation-dependent mechanism, which is common in some studies of carbon-based materials with fluorescent properties. Although the fluorescence of irradiated PMMA array can be tuned by controlling e-beam exposure dose. It is still weak light intensity dependent on the recombination of the π system and surface oxidized defect state. Currently, amine molecules have been regarded as useful and common precursors to enhance the fluorescence efficiency of carbon-based materials [43–46]. The reason for this phenomenon can be explained as follows: (1) The N-doping can increase surface defects. A higher electron-hole recombination core and luminous efficiency can be acquired; (2) The N-doping will provide surface passivation agents to enhance and extend the luminescence time. The corresponding PL spectra are exhibited in Fig. 5b. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of solid carbon-based nanostructure can be tested and calculated by fluorescence spectrophotometer equipment. When sample was excited by 457 nm laser with a power of 150 W, the resulting fluorescence was collected by photomultiplier tube after a series of incident and reflected light paths. Then fluorescence quantum yield of solid carbon structure can be calculated according to formula:
Φ =
(
N f
/
N s
)
×
(
A s
/
A f
)
×
η f
（
N f
is the number of fluorescent photons emitted by the sample;
N s
is the number of excited photons of the sample;
A s
is the intensity of excitation light absorbed by the sample;
A f
is the intensity of fluorescence emitted by the sample;
η f
is the detection efficiency of system）. The calculated maximum mass yield of the irradiated PMMA before and after ammonia treatment are 13.2 % and 64.7 %, respectively.

The dopped sample shows higher luminescence efficiency. Then we adopted NH3·H2O to decorate the irradiated PMMA structures. An obvious enhancement was realized in the dark field image of the sample. The test spectra before (black line) and after (red line) doping also were provided to analyze the changes. In order to study the data on stability of luminescence enhancement after ammonium treatment, we have collected and compared the fluorescence signals of the carbon-based samples treated by ammonia solution at different times. The fluorescence intensity reached the highest level in the initial stage. When the sample was kept in the laboratory for 3 ∼ 5 days, it was found that the intensity of fluorescence signals did not decrease significantly. The decrease of signals may be due to the escape of some unstable ammonia ions in the air environment. We also take the PL mapping for the doped sample. The uniform and regular fluorescence was exhibited, which can provide huge potential for high-resolution space optical sensing applications.

Nano-laser is a hotspot for high-precision optical applications. The luminescence in the nanoscale can be tuned by constructing various low-dimension materials with better spatial resolution and chemical elements [26,47]. MoS2 has a direct gap for optoelectronic device applications. Its luminescence peak is also at about 480 ∼ 500 nm scale, which is similar to the irradiated sample luminescence. The graphitized carbon-based nanostructures were prepared on the surface of MoS2 based on the overlay lithography technology. Fig. 6 a shows the optical image of a composite structure with zero-dimensional carbon dots and two-dimensional film. The corresponding PL mapping image was tested and shown in Fig. 6b. The graphitized carbon structures were distributed on the surface of SiO2/Si substrate and MoS2 film. Pure graphitized carbon materials do not produce fluorescence signals. However, some chemical functional groups including C–H and O–H bonds were remained in carbon-based nanostructure after e-beam irradiation process. The chemical bonds changed the atomic structure of carbon material and the distribution of electronic energy levels, which led to fluorescence phenomenon. Brighter fluorescence signals were presented on carbon-based nanostructure/MoS2 hybrid system. The enhancing phenomenon could be explained by Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Generally, carbon-based nanostructure is easier to generate excited states. Resonance energy can be effectively transferred from carbon-based nanostructure to MoS2 film. Then more photons are excited at the same time, thus enhancing the overall emission intensity. The results show that a higher luminescence intensity was observed in carbon-based nanostructure on the surface of MoS2 film. By further analysis of the spectra of the sample, we find that the luminescence superposition dose existed.

In conclusion, the fabrication of high-resolution solid-carbon PL arrays is important for photophysical study and high-performance sensing devices. Traditional fabrication methods make it difficult to construct arrays with high spatial resolution on the nanometer scale. In this paper, we have developed a new method to prepare carbon-based PL nanostructure. The feature size is as small as 23.89 nm with longitudinal roughness less than 5 nm. A series of fabrication processes were adopted to study the influence of size and exposure dose on PL properties. By controlling the surface molecular state, the PL intensity at the peaks was increased by 17 times because of NH3·H2O solution modification, which exhibits a high sensitivity for sensing or optical applications. Then carbon nano arrays were also fabricated on the surface of semiconductor MoS2 film by overlay lithography process. The PL test results verify a superposition state existed on carbon dots/MoS2 composites. It will provide a reliable method for research on the interfacial effect of graphitic carbon dots and 2D film. As a highly precise and in-situ manufacturing method, the works mentioned in this paper have presented great significance in the optical study of low-dimensional materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52275577, 52205609), Key Research and Development Project Key Program of Shanxi Province, China (202102040201007); General project of Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (grant no. 20210302123056; Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (grant No.2020-109)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of multicolored nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots and their application in polymer self-assemblies

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots: Triggering high-color-purity red fluorescence emission through edge amino protonation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Precisely controlled up/down-conversion liquid and solid state photoluminescence of carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of solid-state carbon dots with intensive emission for white light-emitting devices
